ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists for white puttanesca or guanciale pasta specifically; closest parent 'pasta dish' (FOODON_00002473) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for puttanesca bianca; A00LC (Pasta, cooked) used as closest parent. Dish includes guanciale, olives, anchovies, capers, and olive oil as significant components.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for white puttanesca pasta dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving of white puttanesca with guanciale is approximately 320 g, comprising a full pasta portion with cured pork cheek, olives, anchovies, capers, cherry tomatoes, and olive oil dressing.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta with meat sauce (SR Legacy 21340) and component-level USDA entries for guanciale (cured pork jowl, USDA SR Legacy 10193), olives, anchovies, capers, and olive oil
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for white puttanesca with guanciale. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly: pasta (~55% by weight), guanciale (~12%), taggiasche olives (~8%), anchovies (~5%), capers (~3%), cherry tomatoes (~7%), olive oil (~5%), garlic/lemon/basil (~5%). USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries used for each component. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for a restaurant-style dish of this type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021;114(5):1625–1632. Pasta (cooked al dente, white wheat) GI ≈ 45–49.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for white puttanesca with guanciale. GI estimated from cooked white pasta (al dente) GI = 45 per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, which is the dominant glycemic carbohydrate contributor in this dish. Olive oil, guanciale fat, anchovies, and capers lower the effective GI further; GI 45 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (45 × 68.8) / 100 ≈ 30.9, where carbohydrate per 320 g serving = 21.5 × 3.2 = 68.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from guanciale, anchovies, capers, and olives
hypertension.guidance: A single 320 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1,664 mg sodium, representing 72–83% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size significantly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from pasta carbohydrates, partially offset by fat and protein co-ingestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 31 per serving is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion, substitute wholegrain pasta if available, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from guanciale and elevated sodium from cured and preserved ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Guanciale contributes saturated fat (~2.4 g per 100 g) and the dish's high sodium load from anchovies, capers, and olives warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; occasional consumption in a reduced-portion context is preferable, and the monounsaturated fat from extra virgin olive oil provides a partially offsetting benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White Puttanesca with Guanciale is an Italian pasta dish that reimagines the classic puttanesca by omitting the traditional tomato-based sauce in favour of a lighter, olive-oil-based preparation. The dish features guanciale (cured Italian pork cheek), taggiasche olives, salt-packed anchovies, capers, cherry tomatoes, garlic, lemon juice, fresh basil, and extra virgin olive oil tossed with cooked pasta. The result is a briny, savoury, and umami-rich plate with a silky olive-oil coating rather than a red sauce. Guanciale contributes rendered pork fat and a delicate cured-meat flavour distinct from pancetta or bacon. Taggiasche olives are small, mild Ligurian olives with a buttery texture. The dish is moderately energy-dense at approximately 198 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution from guanciale and anchovies and a significant fat component dominated by heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from olive oil. Sodium content is clinically significant due to the combination of cured pork, anchovies, capers, and olives, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low) based on cooked white pasta as the primary carbohydrate source, though the glycemic load per full restaurant serving is moderate at approximately 31. This dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets and contains gluten and fish allergens.
common_user_queries: How is puttanesca bianca different from regular puttanesca?
How much sodium is in puttanesca pasta?
Calories in white puttanesca with guanciale
What allergens are in puttanesca pasta?

confidence_reason: No exact match for white puttanesca with guanciale exists in any approved primary nutrient database. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for each ingredient. GI is estimated from cooked white pasta (al dente) per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries; no single approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting minor naturally occurring trans fats from the guanciale (ruminant-derived trans fats are not the same as industrial trans fats). 4. GI is estimated at 45 based on cooked white pasta (al dente) as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source per Atkinson et al. 2021; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 5. Sodium is high due to the combination of guanciale, anchovies, capers, and olives; this is a clinically significant feature of the dish. 6. The dish is not halal (contains pork) and not kosher (mixes meat and fish). 7. Monounsaturated fat (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated from olive oil and guanciale components). 8. All -1 sentinel values: vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name from 'White Puttanesca Guanciale' (menu-style) to canonical 'White Puttanesca with Guanciale'.

Set GI to 45 from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables (cooked white pasta al dente) with analogous_estimate documentation; gi_category correctly set to 'low' (GI 45 ≤ 55).
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value in notes as required by G3 sentinel documentation rule.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency; all aliases_by_language strings present verbatim in flat aliases array.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance including quantified sodium load per serving.
Set ontology match_types to 'closest_parent' and 'none' only; no invented codes; gap notes provided for all null/closest_parent entries.
